title: 𒄩𒄠𒈬𒊏𒁉
titleEn: Laws of Hammurabi — Prologue II: the litany of the cities (i 54 – v 25)
part: Prologue II · the litany of the cities (Col. i 54 – v 25)
draftNote: Line numbers restart with each column of the stele (the notes mark where columns ii–v begin). Readings checked sign-by-sign against Harper’s 1904 edition; a few of his readings have been superseded and are flagged in the notes rather than silently modernized.
source: Louvre stele, after R.F. Harper, The Code of Hammurabi, King of Babylon (Chicago, 1904): transliteration pp. 2–9, autograph Plates I–VIII, List of Signs Plates LXXXIII–XCVIII. Readings are Harper’s, written in modern subscript notation; superseded readings kept and flagged (Nanâ/Anunit for Ištar, Kish for Keš, Aleppo for Zabalam, Malka for Malgium, ṣal-tum, nit-ra-ru-šu). Cuneiform in Old Babylonian monumental sign forms via the SantakkuM webfont by Sylvie Vanséveren (CC BY 4.0; hethport.uni-wuerzburg.de/cuneifont/); pronunciation: conventional reconstructed Old Babylonian. Fresh translations, made for this edition directly from the Akkadian, with Harper’s public-domain translation consulted for sense only.
about: The long roll of the cities: from “who heaps up abundance” at the foot of column i, through every great sanctuary of the land — Nippur, Eridu, Babylon, Ur, Sippar, Larsa, Uruk, Isin, Kish, Cutha, Borsippa, Dilbat, Kesh, Lagash, Zabalam, Karkar, Adab, Mashkan-shabri, Malgium, Mari and Tuttul, Agade, Ashur, Nineveh — to the second “am I,” the king’s pedigree, and the coda: “When Marduk commissioned me… I set truth and justice as the speech of the land.” The last word, inūmišu, “at that time,” opens straight onto §1 of the laws. Each epithet is a claim staked in a real city’s temple; the notes flag where Harper’s 1904 readings have since been superseded.

sentences.t:
𒈬𒃵𒈨𒅕
𒉡𒄴𒅆𒅎
𒅇 𒌅𒄴𒁲𒅎
𒈬𒊭𒀝𒇷𒅋
𒈪𒅎𒈠 𒋳𒋗
𒀀𒈾 𒂗𒆤𒆠 𒂦𒀭𒆠
sentences.l: heaper-up of-abundance and of-plenty, completer of-every its-name(=everything) for Nippur (and) Dur-ilu,
sentences.i: who heaps up abundance and plenty, who supplies Nippur and Dur-ilu with every thing there is,

morph.gloss: to bring to completion; D-stem gummurum — mugammer “who heaps up (to the full)”
to make perfect, complete (š-stem of kalûm/kalālu)
anything; mimma šumšu “whatever its name” = everything
Nippur — Enlil’s holy city, written EN.LÍL{ki}
Dur-ilu (Der) — “Fortress of the god,” east-Tigris cult city

sentences.n: The litany begins: from here to the top of column v the king unrolls a chain of epithets — mostly mu- participles, “he who …-s” — city by city across the whole land. mimma šumšu, literally “whatever its name,” is Akkadian for “everything.” Nippur is written EN.LÍL{ki}: Enlil’s own name serves as his city’s.

sentences.t:
𒍝𒉌𒉡𒌝
𒈾𒄴𒁺𒌝
𒊭 𒂍𒆳
𒈗𒌝 𒇷𒄿𒀀𒌝
sentences.l: the-provider pious of Ekur, the-king capable,
sentences.i: the pious provider of Ekur, the able king,

morph.gloss: to provision; zāninum “provider” — the royal temple-keeper’s title
Ekur, “Mountain House” — Enlil’s temple at Nippur

sentences.n: zāninum “provider” is the royal epithet par excellence for temple upkeep. Ekur, “Mountain House,” is Enlil’s temple at Nippur. lēʾûm “capable, competent” — the same word Harper renders “powerful.”

sentences.l: returner of-Eridu to its-place, purifier of
sentences.i: who restored Eridu to its place, who purifies

morph.gloss: to return; D-stem turrum — mutēr “who gives back”
Eridu — the first city, written NUN{ki}; Ea’s seat
to be pure; D-stem ubbubum — mubbib “who purifies”

sentences.n: Eridu — the first city of the King List — is written NUN{ki}, exactly as there. The sentence runs over the column edge: its object stands at the top of column ii.

sentences.i: the purification-rites of E-abzu; the storm of the four quarters of the world;

morph.gloss: E-abzu, “House of the Deep” — Ea’s temple at Eridu (written É.ZU.AB)
the world-quarters, edges of the world

sentences.n: Column ii begins. E-abzu is Ea’s temple of the Deep at Eridu — written É.ZU.AB, the signs of the abzu reversed, as the scribes liked it. Then the register flips: from priestly purity straight to tīb kibrāt erbettim, “the onslaught of the four world-quarters.”

sentences.i: who makes the name of Babylon great,
who gladdens the heart of Marduk his lord,

morph.gloss: to be good, pleasing; D-stem ṭubbum “make good”; muṭīb “gladdener”

sentences.l: who daily stands (in service) for Esagila,
sentences.i: who stands daily in the service of Esagila,

morph.gloss: Esagila, “House whose top is high” — Marduk’s temple in Babylon

sentences.n: Esagila, “the house whose top is high,” is Marduk’s temple in Babylon. ūmišu “daily” is written with the sun-sign UD plus phonetic -mi-šu — tap the UD to see its many lives: ud, ut, tam, ūmu “day,” and (with the divine marker) Šamaš himself.

sentences.i: seed of kingship, whom Sin himself created,

morph.gloss: seed; offspring (written with the seed-sign NUMUN)
Sin ({d}EN.ZU) — the moon-god, patron of Ur
to build, create; ibniušu “whom he created”

sentences.n: zēr šarrūtim — the “seed of kingship” — is written with the NUMUN sign, the seed-grain itself (Harper transliterates zêr). Sin, the moon-god, is written EN.ZU: the signs of his Sumerian name zu-en, reversed on the stone. This claims the moon-god as the dynasty’s ancestor — Sin is patron of Ur, the next city named.

sentences.i: who makes Ur prosper,

morph.gloss: to prosper; D-stem — munaḫḫiš “who makes prosper”

sentences.n: Ur — ŠEŠ.AB{ki}, the city of the moon-god (modern editions read the signs ŠEŠ.UNUG). The {uru} in front is the silent city-marker, as {d} is for gods: tap it.

sentences.t:
𒉿𒀸𒊒𒌝
𒈬𒍑𒋼𒈪𒄣
𒁀𒉋 𒃶𒃲𒅎
𒀀𒈾 𒂍𒆧𒉡𒃲
sentences.l: the-humble, the-devout, bringer of-abundance to Ekishnugal,
sentences.i: humble and devout, who brings abundance to Ekishnugal,

morph.gloss: abundance (Sumerian ḫé-gál, written with its own signs)
Ekishnugal — Sin’s temple at Ur (Harper: É.NER.NU.GAL)

sentences.n: ḫegallum “abundance” is the Sumerian ḫé-gál, “let there be!”, borrowed whole into Akkadian and still written with its Sumerian signs. Ekishnugal is Sin’s temple at Ur; Harper read its signs É.NER.NU.GAL.

sentences.i: the discerning king, obedient to mighty Shamash,

morph.gloss: to hear, obey; šēmû “obedient (to)”; Š-stem muštešmi “who makes obey”
Shamash ({d}UTU) — the sun-god, lord of justice
strong, mighty (dan written with the KAL sign)

sentences.i: who made the foundations of Sippar firm,

morph.gloss: to be firm; D-stem kunnum — mukīn “who makes firm”
foundation (written with the single sign SUḪUŠ)

sentences.n: išid “foundation” is written with a single sign (Harper reads išid; the sign is the classic SUḪUŠ). Sippar, city of the sun-god, is written UD.KIB.NUN — the KIB is two crossed wood-signs, a rare shape you can spot at once.

sentences.i: who clothes the sacred terrace of Aja in green,

morph.gloss: the gigunû — the terraced, tree-planted shrine
Aja ({d}A.A) — bride of Shamash, the Dawn

sentences.n: A garden image: the gigunû is the tree-planted terrace-shrine; “clothing it in green” (warqum) means keeping it growing. Aja — written with two plain A-signs — is the sun-god’s bride; Harper took the name for “Malkat.”

sentences.t:
𒈬𒍣𒅕
𒂍 𒂍𒌓𒊏
𒊭 𒆠 𒋗𒁀𒀜 𒊭𒈠𒄿
sentences.l: decorator of the-temple Ebabbar, which (is) like the-dwelling of-heaven,
sentences.i: who adorned the temple of Ebabbar, which is like a dwelling of heaven,

morph.gloss: like, as (the short form of kīma)

sentences.n: Ebabbar, “Shining House,” Shamash’s temple at Sippar — the shine written with the sun-sign itself (BABBAR “white/bright” = UD). Note bīt Ebabbarra: É twice, first as the word “temple,” then opening the temple’s own name.

sentences.i: the warrior who spared Larsa,

morph.gloss: to spare, deal kindly; gāmil “sparer of”; igmilu “who spared”
Larsa (UD.UNU{ki}) — the sun-god’s southern city, Rim-Sin’s seat

sentences.n: To “spare” (gamālum) Larsa is pointed politeness: Hammurabi conquered it, from his great rival Rim-Sin. Larsa is the sun-god’s other city — written UD plus the sign Harper read UNU (modern editions: UNUG).

sentences.i: who renewed Ebabbar for Shamash his helper,

morph.gloss: to be new; D-stem uddušum — muddiš “renewer”
Shamash ({d}UTU) — the sun-god, lord of justice

sentences.i: the lord who gave life to Uruk,

morph.gloss: to live; D-stem bulluṭum — muballiṭ “who gives life”
Uruk (UNU{ki}) — city of Anum and Ishtar

sentences.n: Uruk — Gilgamesh’s city — written with the single sign Harper read UNU (modern editions: UNUG).

sentences.i: who provides abundant waters for its people,

morph.gloss: to place, establish; šākin “who provides”; aškun “I established”

sentences.i: who raised high the summit of Eanna,

morph.gloss: to go up; D-stem ullûm — mulli “who raises high”
Eanna, “House of Heaven” — the great temple of Uruk

sentences.n: Eanna, “House of Heaven” — the great temple of Anum and Ishtar at Uruk.

sentences.t:
𒈬𒃵𒈨𒅕
𒄭𒄑𒁉𒅎
𒀀𒈾 𒀭𒀀𒉡
𒅇 𒀭𒈹
sentences.l: heaper-up of-plenty for Anum and Ishtar,
sentences.i: who heaps up plenty for Anum and Ishtar,

morph.gloss: to bring to completion; D-stem gummurum — mugammer “who heaps up (to the full)”
Ishtar — goddess of love and war; written {d}INANNA (Harper: Nanâ) or GIŠ.DAR (Harper: Anunit)

sentences.n: The goddess is written with the single INANNA sign; Harper reads it Nanâ, and elsewhere renders her Anunit — all faces of Ishtar. Here she and Anum share Eanna.

sentences.t:
𒀭𒌆 𒈠𒁴
𒈬𒉺𒄴𒄭𒅕
𒉌𒅆 𒊓𒀊𒄩𒁴
𒊭 𒉌𒋛𒅔𒆠
sentences.l: the-canopy of-the-land, gatherer of-the-people scattered of Isin,
sentences.i: the protecting canopy of the land, who gathered the scattered people of Isin,

morph.gloss: Isin (NI.SI.IN{ki}) — seat of the dynasty before Babylon’s rise

sentences.n: ṣalūlum is the shade-canopy — the king as shelter over the land, written AN.DUL₃. Isin had been the seat of a rival dynasty; “gathering its scattered people” is restoration talk.

sentences.i: who lavishes abundance on the temple Egalmah,

morph.gloss: to be lavish; D-stem — mudaḫḫid “who makes lavish”
Egalmah, “Exalted Palace” — Gula’s temple at Isin

sentences.n: Egalmaḫ, “Exalted Palace” — the temple of the healing goddess Gula at Isin.

sentences.l: the-dragon of-kings(?), king of-the-city, own-brother of-Zamama,
sentences.i: the dragon among kings, the city-king, own brother of Zamama,

morph.gloss: the ušumgal-dragon (GAL.BÚR); of kings: matchless overlord
“king of the city” — Harper’s reading of the recurring pair LUGAL.URU-lim
Zamama ({d}ZA.MAL.MAL; modern Zababa) — warrior-god of Kish

sentences.n: ušumgallum is the ušumgal — the great serpent-dragon — written GAL.BÚR as Harper’s own sign-list explains; of kings it means “matchless overlord.” Zamama is the warrior-god of Kish (modern editions spell him za-ba₄-ba₄); talīmum is the favorite full brother. Harper linked šar+alim into one phrase, “the city king.”

sentences.i: who made the dwelling-place of Kish firm,

morph.gloss: to found; Š-stem — mušaršid “who founds firmly”
Kish (KIŠ{ki}) — ancient royal city, Zamama’s seat

sentences.n: Kish — the city whose name the King List gives to the first dynasty after the Flood — written with the same KIŠ sign that spells kiššatum “totality” in part one (Harper reads it NER here).

sentences.i: who wrapped Emeteursag in fearsome radiance,

morph.gloss: to go around; Š-stem — muštašḫir “who wraps around”
the awe-inspiring radiance of gods and kings
Emeteursag, “House worthy of the hero” — Zamama’s temple at Kish

sentences.n: melammum is the numinous glow around gods and true kings — “radiance” is the pale English for it. Emeteursag, “House worthy of the hero,” is Zamama’s temple at Kish.

sentences.i: who arranges duly the great rites of Ishtar,

morph.gloss: to arrange duly; muštēṣbi “who arranges in order”
Ishtar — goddess of love and war; written {d}INANNA (Harper: Nanâ) or GIŠ.DAR (Harper: Anunit)

sentences.t:
𒉺𒆠𒀉 𒂍𒁴
𒄯𒊕𒌦𒈠
𒂍 𒆦 𒈾𒆠𒊑
sentences.l: caretaker of-the-temple Hursag-kalama, the-house of-the-court of-enemies,
sentences.i: caretaker of the temple of Hursag-kalama, that walled court of the enemy,

morph.gloss: Hursag-kalama, “Mountain of the Land” — Kish’s twin sanctuary of Ishtar

sentences.n: Ḫursagkalama, “Mountain of the Land,” is Kish’s twin sanctuary. The phrase bīt kisal nakirī is a real puzzle — Harper made it “the grave of the enemy”; word by word it is “house of the courtyard of enemies,” perhaps where beaten enemies were mustered. The notes cannot make it smoother than the stone does.

sentences.i: whose helper let him attain

morph.gloss: helper(?) — Harper’s reading; modern editions see the god Erra, the king’s ally, in these signs
to reach, attain; Š-stem ušakšidu “he let attain”; ikšudu “who reached”

sentences.n: A crux. Harper read nit-ra-ru-šu, “whose help brings victory”; modern editions see here the war-god Erra as the king’s ally (ša Erra rūṣūšu). The object — “his desire” — waits at the top of column iii.

sentences.i: his heart’s desire. He who made Cutha surpassing,

morph.gloss: to exceed; Š-stem — mušāter “who makes surpassing”; ušāterūšu “they made it supreme”
Cutha ({uru}TIG.GAB.A{ki}, modern reading Gudua) — Nergal’s city of the underworld

sentences.n: Column iii begins mid-thought: nizmassu “his desire” completes the clause left hanging at the foot of column ii. Cutha is the city of Nergal, lord of the underworld; Harper read the signs TIG.GAB.A (modern editions: GU₂.DU₈.A).

sentences.i: who enlarged the Meslam temple with everything there is,

morph.gloss: to be broad; D-stem — murappiš “who broadens”
anything; mimma šumšu “whatever its name” = everything
the Meslam — Nergal’s temple at Cutha (Harper: Shid-lam)

sentences.n: The Meslam is Nergal’s temple in Cutha. Harper read its signs ŠID.LAM (“Shid-lam”); the first sign is today read MES — same sign, later value.

sentences.i: the fierce wild bull who gores the foes,
sentences.n: The rīmum is the aurochs, the untamable wild bull — the royal animal of irresistible force. Harper printed ka-at-ru-um; the word is kadrum “fierce, rampant.”

sentences.i: beloved of Tutu, who makes Borsippa rejoice,

morph.gloss: Tutu — god of Borsippa, later a name of Marduk
to rejoice; D-stem — murīš “who makes rejoice”

sentences.n: Tutu is the god of Borsippa, Babylon’s sister-city — his name later absorbed into Marduk’s. Two plain TU-signs write him.

sentences.i: the pious one, never ceasing in care for Ezida,

morph.gloss: Ezida, “True House” — the temple of Borsippa

sentences.n: lā mupparkûm — “un-ceasing” — one long negative participle. Ezida, “True House,” is Tutu’s temple at Borsippa.

sentences.i: god among city-kings, who knows wisdom,

morph.gloss: “king of the city” — Harper’s reading of the recurring pair LUGAL.URU-lim
to know; mūdi “knower of”; īdû “he knows”

sentences.n: igigallum “wisdom” is another Sumerian loan spelled with its own signs, IGI.GÁL, “the eye that is open.”

sentences.i: who extends the cultivated lands of Dilbat,

morph.gloss: to be wide; D-stem — mušaddil “who widens”
Dilbat (AŠ.BAD{ki}) — farming town south of Babylon, Urash’s city

sentences.n: Dilbat, south of Babylon, was farm country — its blessing is more ploughland. The city’s name is written DIL (the single upright wedge) + BAT.

sentences.i: who heaps up the grain-piles for mighty Urash,

morph.gloss: to pile up; D-stem — mugarrin “who heaps up”

sentences.n: Urash, the farmer-god of Dilbat, is written with the IB sign — Harper marks his own reading with a “(?)”. The karû are the open grain-heaps of harvest.

sentences.t:
𒁁𒈝 𒍣𒈠𒀜
𒄩𒀜𒁲𒅎
𒅇 𒀀𒄀𒅎
𒊭 𒌑𒊭𒀝𒇷𒇻𒋗
𒂊𒊑𒅖𒌈
𒀭𒈠𒈠
sentences.l: the-lord, ornament of-scepter and of-crown, whom made-perfect-him the-wise-one Mama,
sentences.i: the lord, true ornament of scepter and crown, whom wise Mama made perfect,

morph.gloss: what befits; simat “the fitting ornament of”
to make perfect, complete (š-stem of kalûm/kalālu)
the wise one (fem.) — epithet of the mother-goddess
Mama ({d}MA.MA) — the mother-goddess who forms kings

sentences.n: simat ḫaṭṭim u agîm — “what befits scepter and crown.” Mama (two MA-signs) is the mother-goddess who forms kings in the womb; erištum “the wise one” is her fixed epithet.

sentences.i: who set firm the sacred designs of Kesh,

morph.gloss: to be firm; D-stem kunnum — mukīn “who makes firm”
Kesh — holy city of the mother-goddess (same KIŠ sign; Harper read Kish)

sentences.n: uṣurātum are the “drawings”: the divinely fixed plans of a sanctuary. The sign is again KIŠ; Harper read the city as Kish, but Mama’s holy town is Keš — the same sign carries both names.

sentences.i: who lavishes pure foods on Nintu,

morph.gloss: to sprout abundantly; D-stem — mudešši “who supplies richly”

sentences.n: Nintu, “Lady of Birth,” is another face of the mother-goddess just praised as Mama.

sentences.t:
𒈬𒍑𒋫𒈝
𒄀𒀉𒈠𒈝
𒊭𒄿𒅎
𒈪𒊑𒁴
𒅇 𒈠𒀸𒆠𒁴
𒀀𒈾 𒋓𒁓𒆷𒆠
𒅇 𒄈𒋢𒆠
sentences.l: the-deliberate, the-perfect, determiner of-pasture and watering-place for Lagash and Girsu,
sentences.i: judicious and perfect, who appoints pasture and watering-place for Lagash and Girsu,

morph.gloss: judicious, deliberate (one who takes counsel with himself)
Lagash (ŠIR.BUR.LA{ki}) — great city of the southeast

sentences.n: šāʾim — “who determines” — is the same verb the gods used on destiny in line 1 of the monument; here the king determines where flocks graze and drink. Lagash and its temple-city Girsu lie in the far southeast.

sentences.i: who maintains the great food-offerings for Eninnu,

morph.gloss: Eninnu, “House Fifty” (É.50) — Ningirsu’s temple at Girsu

sentences.n: Eninnu, Ningirsu’s temple at Girsu, is written É.50 — “House Fifty,” with the actual numeral, five tens, carved on the stone. Fifty is Ningirsu’s (and Enlil’s) sacred number.

sentences.i: who grips the enemies fast, favorite of the Capable Goddess,

morph.gloss: the Capable One — a title of Ishtar

sentences.n: telītum, “the capable, the competent one,” is a standing title of Ishtar — Harper guessed “the exalted god (oracle?)”. Her favor and a fist full of enemies sit comfortably in one breath here.

sentences.i: who fulfils the oracles of Zabalam,

morph.gloss: to make perfect, complete (š-stem of kalûm/kalālu)
Zabalam — Ishtar’s city in the south (Harper copied ṢA.RI.UNU and thought of Aleppo)

sentences.n: têrētum are oracular instructions — divine orders read from omens. Harper copied the signs ṢA.RI.UNU and thought of Aleppo; the city is Zabalam, a seat of Ishtar in the south.

sentences.i: who gladdens the heart of Ishtar,

morph.gloss: Ishtar — goddess of love and war; written {d}INANNA (Harper: Nanâ) or GIŠ.DAR (Harper: Anunit)

sentences.n: Here the goddess is spelled with two signs, GIŠ.DAR — the old writing iš₈-tár — where elsewhere the single INANNA sign serves. Same goddess, two garments; Harper renders this one “Anunit.”

sentences.t:
𒊒𒁍𒌝 𒂖𒈝
𒊭 𒉌𒅖 𒂵𒋾𒋗
𒀭𒅎 𒄿𒁺𒌑
sentences.l: the-prince pure, whose lifting of-his-hands Adad knows,
sentences.i: the pure prince, whose lifted hands Adad acknowledges,

morph.gloss: raising, lifting; nīš qātim “prayer” (lifting of hands)
to know; mūdi “knower of”; īdû “he knows”

sentences.n: nīš qātim, “the lifting of the hands,” is the very word for prayer. Adad, the storm-god, is written {d}IM — the same IM that spells the syllable and the wind.

sentences.t:
𒈬𒉈𒄴
𒇷𒅁𒁉 𒀭𒅎
𒆪𒊏𒁲𒅎
𒄿𒈾 𒌷𒅎𒆠
sentences.l: quieter of-the-heart of-Adad the-warrior in Karkar,
sentences.i: who stills the heart of Adad the warrior in Karkar,
sentences.n: Three IM-signs in three roles within two lines: the god ({d}IM), the syllable, and the city ({uru}IM{ki}, Karkar) — cuneiform economy at its most mischievous.

sentences.i: who set the proper emblems in E-udgalgal,

morph.gloss: to place, establish; šākin “who provides”; aškun “I established”
what befits; simat “the fitting ornament of”
E-udgalgal, “House of Great Storms” — Adad’s temple at Karkar

sentences.n: E-udgalgal, “House of Great Storms” — Adad’s temple at Karkar.

sentences.i: the king who gave life back to Adab,

morph.gloss: Adab (UD.NUN{ki}) — city of the mother-goddess as Ninmah

sentences.i: who cares for the temple Emah,

morph.gloss: to organize, care for; āšer “who looks after”
Emah, “Exalted House” — the temple at Adab

sentences.n: Emaḫ, “Exalted House,” at Adab — the mother-goddess again, this time as Ninmah.

sentences.i: lordliest of city-kings, a fighter none can face,

morph.gloss: “king of the city” — Harper’s reading of the recurring pair LUGAL.URU-lim
to face, withstand; qabal lā maḫārim “combat none can face”

sentences.n: qabal lā maḫārim — “combat that cannot be met.” Column iii closes on the drumbeat; column iv opens with mercy.

sentences.i: he who granted life to Mashkan-shabri,
sentences.n: Column iv. The town’s name ends in the Sumerogram PA.AL — the šabra-official — so Harper read Mashkan-shabri, “settlement of the steward.”

sentences.i: who lets abundance flow for the Meslam,

morph.gloss: to give to drink; Š-stem — mušešqi “who waters”
the Meslam — Nergal’s temple at Cutha (Harper: Shid-lam)

sentences.t:
𒅎𒄣 𒈬𒋰𒁉𒈝
𒋗
𒅅𒋗𒁺
𒈾𒂵𒀊 𒌨𒅆𒅎
sentences.l: the-wise, the-capable-manager, he-who reached the-source of-wisdom,
sentences.i: wise and able in affairs, who has reached the very source of wisdom,

morph.gloss: capable manager, one who gets things done
to reach, attain; Š-stem ušakšidu “he let attain”; ikšudu “who reached”

sentences.n: nagab uršim, “the spring of wisdom” — Harper, memorably, made it “the bandit caves(?)”. Both took the same signs; only one took the king’s point.

sentences.i: who sheltered the people of Malka in catastrophe,

morph.gloss: to shelter, hide away; mušpazzir “who gives refuge”
Malka (MAL.AL.KA.A{ki}) — Harper’s reading; the city is Malgium on the Tigris

sentences.n: karašûm is annihilating disaster. Harper read the town MAL.AL.KA.A as Malka; modern editions know it as Malgium, on the Tigris — a city Hammurabi in fact destroyed. Sheltering its people is the victor’s version.

sentences.i: who settled them in dwellings of plenty,

morph.gloss: to found; Š-stem — mušaršid “who founds firmly”

sentences.n: The last word should end -im; the carver wrote -in, and Harper prints the slip as it stands — nu-uḫ-ši-in(=im). Even the king’s stone has typos.

sentences.t:
𒀀𒈾 𒀭𒂗𒆠
𒅇 𒀭𒁮𒃲𒉣𒈾
𒈬𒊬𒁍𒌑
𒊬𒊒𒋾𒋗
𒁕𒌷𒅖 𒄿𒅆𒈬
𒍣𒁉 𒂖𒇻𒁴
sentences.l: for Ea and Damgalnunna — magnifiers of-his-kingship — forever he-decreed offerings pure,
sentences.i: who decreed for ever pure offerings for Ea and Damgalnunna, who magnify his kingship,

morph.gloss: Ea ({d}EN.KI) — god of wisdom and the deep
Damgalnunna ({d}DAM.GAL.NUN.NA) — “Great Wife of the Prince,” Ea’s consort

sentences.n: A ring closing: Ea and his queen Damgalnunna magnify the king’s rule, and he binds himself to feed them for ever — dāriš, the adverb of the dynasty’s favorite word.

sentences.i: first among city-kings, who bent the settlements of the Euphrates to his will,

morph.gloss: “king of the city” — Harper’s reading of the recurring pair LUGAL.URU-lim

sentences.n: The Euphrates wears the silent river-marker {íd} — the water-sign plus the deep — before the same signs that write Sippar, UD.KIB.NUN, here read Purattum, the river itself.

sentences.i: the warrior of Dagan, his creator,

morph.gloss: warrior(?) — Harper’s reading of a disputed word (also read ittum “by the sign of”)
Dagan — great god of the middle Euphrates

sentences.n: Dagan is the great god of the middle Euphrates — the region of the next two towns. The first word is a crux: Harper read ṣal-tum, “the warrior(?)”; the signs have also been read as ittum, “by the sign (of Dagan)”. The stone keeps its counsel.

sentences.t:
𒋗 𒅅𒈪𒇻
𒉌𒅆 𒈨𒊏𒆠
𒅇 𒌅𒌅𒌌𒆠
sentences.l: he-who spared the-people of-Mera and Tuttul,
sentences.i: who spared the people of Mari and Tuttul,

morph.gloss: to spare, deal kindly; gāmil “sparer of”; igmilu “who spared”
Mera ({uru}ME.RA{ki}) — Mari, the great palace-city of the Euphrates

sentences.n: Mera is Mari, the great palace-city of the middle Euphrates — another rival Hammurabi defeated and, the stele prefers to say, “spared.” Tuttul is Dagan’s cult-town at the Baliḫ mouth.

sentences.i: the pious prince, who brightens the face of Ishtar,

morph.gloss: to be bright; D-stem — munawwir “who brightens”
Ishtar — goddess of love and war; written {d}INANNA (Harper: Nanâ) or GIŠ.DAR (Harper: Anunit)

sentences.i: who sets out pure foods for Ninazu,

morph.gloss: to place, establish; šākin “who provides”; aškun “I established”

sentences.n: Ninazu — “Lord Healer,” an underworld god, whose name the signs spell out: NIN “lord/lady,” A.ZU “the water-knower,” the physician.

sentences.i: who saves his people in the tight place,
sentences.n: pušqum is the narrows — distress as a defile you must be pulled out of.

sentences.i: who establishes their portion within Babylon in peace,

morph.gloss: to be firm; D-stem kunnum — mukīn “who makes firm”

sentences.n: The -iš ending of šulmāniš makes an adverb of šulmānum: “peace-wise.” You have seen it in dāriš “for ever” — a suffix worth collecting.

sentences.t:
𒉺𒇻 𒉌𒅊
𒊭 𒅁𒊺𒌅𒋗
𒂊𒇷 𒄑𒁯 𒁕𒁀
sentences.l: shepherd of-the-people, whose deeds to Ishtar are-pleasing,
sentences.i: shepherd of the people, whose deeds please Ishtar,

morph.gloss: shepherd — here written with the Sumerogram SIPA
Ishtar — goddess of love and war; written {d}INANNA (Harper: Nanâ) or GIŠ.DAR (Harper: Anunit)
to be good, pleasing; D-stem ṭubbum “make good”; muṭīb “gladdener”

sentences.n: The shepherd returns from column i — this time written with the Sumerogram SIPA. And watch the ši of nišī: it is the gunû-form of the eye-sign, IGI with extra strokes; Harper marks it ši(g).

sentences.t:
𒈬𒆠𒅔𒉌 𒄑𒁯
𒄿𒈾 𒂍𒌌𒈦
𒄈𒁍𒌝
𒀀𒂵𒉈𒆠
𒊑𒁉𒁴
sentences.l: who-installed Ishtar in Eulmash within Agade of-the-broad-street,
sentences.i: who installed Ishtar in Eulmash, in Agade of the wide squares,

morph.gloss: to be firm; D-stem kunnum — mukīn “who makes firm”
Ishtar — goddess of love and war; written {d}INANNA (Harper: Nanâ) or GIŠ.DAR (Harper: Anunit)

sentences.n: Agade — Akkad, Sargon’s lost capital, spelled a-ga-dè. Its Ishtar is Annunitum, which is exactly whom Harper names here. rībītum is the broad public way; “Agade of the thoroughfare” was a stock phrase.

sentences.i: who brings truth to light and guides the people aright,

morph.gloss: to appear; Š-stem šūpûm “make appear, make glorious”; mušēpi, ušūpiu
truth, right — kittum u mīšarum, “truth and justice”
to be straight; Št-stem šutēšurum “to guide aright”; mušūšer
the people (a rare, archaic word; Harper am-mi)

sentences.n: Two verbs that will return in the coda: making truth appear (the š-stem of wapûm, as in šūpîm of column i) and setting people on the straight path (šutēšurum). The laws themselves are being foreshadowed.

sentences.i: who returned its gracious guardian spirit to Ashur,

morph.gloss: to return; D-stem turrum — mutēr “who gives back”
the lamassu — protective guardian spirit (the KAL sign)
Ashur ({uru}A.USAR{ki}) — the city on the Tigris, later Assyria’s heart

sentences.n: The lamassu is the protective spirit — written with the KAL sign, which doubles as dan “strong” and, with the divine marker, the guardian herself. Ashur is spelled in the old way Harper prints A.USAR.

sentences.i: who makes the radiance shine(?),

morph.gloss: to make shine(?) — Harper’s mu-še-ib-bi; a disputed line
radiance(?), rising light(?) — Harper renders “the rising sun(?)”

sentences.n: A one-line puzzle Harper marks with his own “(?)” — “who made the rising sun(?) shine.” The next lines say where: Ishtar’s house in Nineveh.

sentences.t:
𒈗𒌝 𒊭 𒄿𒈾 𒉌𒉡𒀀𒆠
𒄿𒈾 𒂍𒋃𒋃
𒌑𒋗𒁉𒌑
𒈨𒂊 𒀭𒈹
sentences.l: the-king who in Nineveh, in Emishmish, made-glorious the-rites of-Ishtar,
sentences.i: the king who in Nineveh, in the temple Emishmish, made the rites of Ishtar glorious,

morph.gloss: Emishmish (É.MIŠ.MIŠ; Emesmes/Emašmaš) — Ishtar’s temple at Nineveh
to appear; Š-stem šūpûm “make appear, make glorious”; mušēpi, ušūpiu
the me — the divine rites and powers (me-e)
Ishtar — goddess of love and war; written {d}INANNA (Harper: Nanâ) or GIŠ.DAR (Harper: Anunit)

sentences.n: Nineveh, of all places — centuries before Assyrian kings returned the favor by carrying this stele’s laws into their libraries. Emishmish (Emesmes, later Emašmaš) is Ishtar-of-Nineveh’s temple; its MIŠ signs are the ŠID of Meslam again.

sentences.i: pious, devout in prayer before the great gods,
sentences.n: ilāni rabûti “the great gods” is written by doubling the signs — god-god, great-great — cuneiform’s plainest way of saying “many.”

sentences.t:
𒇷𒅁𒇷𒅁𒁉
𒊭 𒋢𒈬𒆷𒀭
𒌉𒆗𒉏
𒊭 𒀭𒂗𒍪𒈬𒁀𒇷𒀉
sentences.l: great-grandson of Sumula-il, son mighty of Sin-muballit,
sentences.i: descendant of Sumula-El, mighty son of Sin-muballit,
sentences.n: The pedigree: Sumula-El founded the dynasty of Babylon; Sin-muballit was Hammurabi’s father — his name, “Sin gives life,” written with the moon-god’s EN.ZU. liblibbum is the “heart-of-heart”: offspring of offspring.

sentences.i: the eternal seed of kingship,

morph.gloss: seed; offspring (written with the seed-sign NUMUN)

sentences.n: Column v: the peroration. The seed-sign NUMUN from column ii returns, now for the dynasty itself.

sentences.i: the mighty king, the very Sun of Babylon,

morph.gloss: strong, mighty (dan written with the KAL sign)
Shamash ({d}UTU) — the sun-god, lord of justice

sentences.n: The boldest title on the stele: the king written as {d}UTU — Shamash — of Babylon. On the relief above these words, Hammurabi stands receiving the rod and ring from that same sun-god.

sentences.t:
𒈬𒊺𒍣 𒉡𒊑𒅎
𒀀𒈾 𒈠𒀜
𒋗𒈨𒅕𒅎
𒅇 𒀝𒅗𒁲𒅎
sentences.l: who-makes-go-out light over the-land of-Sumer and of-Akkad,
sentences.i: who makes light rise over the land of Sumer and Akkad,

morph.gloss: to go out; (of light) to rise; Š-stem mušēṣi “who makes go forth”

sentences.n: “Sumer and Akkad” — the old double name of the whole river-country, here spelled out syllable by syllable. The image completes the Sun-of-Babylon title: what the sun does, the king does.

sentences.i: the king who makes the four quarters of the world obey,

morph.gloss: to hear, obey; šēmû “obedient (to)”; Š-stem muštešmi “who makes obey”
the world-quarters, edges of the world

sentences.i: the favorite of Ishtar — am I.

morph.gloss: Ishtar — goddess of love and war; written {d}INANNA (Harper: Nanâ) or GIŠ.DAR (Harper: Anunit)

sentences.n: The second anāku. The whole litany, from the shepherd of column i to the Sun of Babylon, has hung on the “I am” of i 53 — and lands on this one.

sentences.t:
𒄿𒉡𒈠
𒀭𒀫𒌓
𒀀𒈾 𒋗𒋼𒋗𒌨 𒉌𒅆
𒌦 𒌑𒋛𒅎
𒋗𒄷𒍣𒅎
𒌑𒉿𒂊𒊏𒀭𒉌
sentences.l: When Marduk to set-aright the-people, the-land right-conduct to-make-take-hold, commissioned-me,
sentences.i: When Marduk commissioned me to guide the people aright and to give the land right conduct,

morph.gloss: to be straight; Št-stem šutēšurum “to guide aright”; mušūšer
to take, grasp; Š-stem šūḫuzum “to make take hold”
to go; D-stem wu’’urum “to commission”: uwaʾʾeranni “he commissioned me”

sentences.n: The coda begins exactly as the monument began — a when-clause — but now the commission is stated plainly: šutēšur nišī, to set the people on the straight way. uwaʾʾeranni ends in -anni, “me”: the king again the object of a god’s verb.

sentences.t:
𒆠𒀉𒌓
𒅇 𒈪𒊭𒊏𒄠
𒄿𒈾 𒅗 𒈠𒁴
𒀸𒆪𒌦
sentences.l: truth and justice in the-mouth of-the-land I-placed,
sentences.i: I set truth and justice as the very speech of the land,

morph.gloss: truth, right — kittum u mīšarum, “truth and justice”
mouth (the KA sign); ina pī mātim “in the mouth of the land”
to place, establish; šākin “who provides”; aškun “I established”

sentences.n: The sentence the whole prologue exists to say — and the first main verb in the king’s own voice: aškun, “I placed.” kittum is truth as firm ground; mīšarum is justice as fairness; ina pī mātim, “in the mouth of the land,” written with the mouth-sign KA itself.

sentences.i: and made the people prosper.

morph.gloss: to be good, pleasing; D-stem ṭubbum “make good”; muṭīb “gladdener”

sentences.n: šīr nišī ṭubbum — the phrase from i 47-48, now in the first person, completed: what the gods called him to do, he reports done.

sentences.i: At that time:

morph.gloss: “at that time” — the hinge-word of the monument

sentences.n: The prologue’s last word, hanging like a colon. What follows it on the stone is §1 of the laws: “If a man accuse a man…” — the door the whole poem has been walking toward.

glossary.gamārum: to bring to completion; D-stem gummurum — mugammer “who heaps up (to the full)”
glossary.šuklulum: to make perfect, complete (š-stem of kalûm/kalālu)
glossary.mimma: anything; mimma šumšu “whatever its name” = everything
glossary.Nippur: Nippur — Enlil’s holy city, written EN.LÍL{ki}
glossary.Dūr-ilim: Dur-ilu (Der) — “Fortress of the god,” east-Tigris cult city
glossary.zanānum: to provision; zāninum “provider” — the royal temple-keeper’s title
glossary.Ekur: Ekur, “Mountain House” — Enlil’s temple at Nippur
glossary.târum: to return; D-stem turrum — mutēr “who gives back”
glossary.Eridu: Eridu — the first city, written NUN{ki}; Ea’s seat
glossary.ebēbum: to be pure; D-stem ubbubum — mubbib “who purifies”
glossary.E-abzu: E-abzu, “House of the Deep” — Ea’s temple at Eridu (written É.ZU.AB)
glossary.kibrātum: the world-quarters, edges of the world
glossary.ṭâbum: to be good, pleasing; D-stem ṭubbum “make good”; muṭīb “gladdener”
glossary.Esagila: Esagila, “House whose top is high” — Marduk’s temple in Babylon
glossary.zērum: seed; offspring (written with the seed-sign NUMUN)
glossary.Sin: Sin ({d}EN.ZU) — the moon-god, patron of Ur
glossary.banûm: to build, create; ibniušu “whom he created”
glossary.naḫāšum: to prosper; D-stem — munaḫḫiš “who makes prosper”
glossary.ḫegallum: abundance (Sumerian ḫé-gál, written with its own signs)
glossary.Ekišnugal: Ekishnugal — Sin’s temple at Ur (Harper: É.NER.NU.GAL)
glossary.šemûm: to hear, obey; šēmû “obedient (to)”; Š-stem muštešmi “who makes obey”
glossary.Šamaš: Shamash ({d}UTU) — the sun-god, lord of justice
glossary.dannum: strong, mighty (dan written with the KAL sign)
glossary.kânum: to be firm; D-stem kunnum — mukīn “who makes firm”
glossary.išdum: foundation (written with the single sign SUḪUŠ)
glossary.gigunûm: the gigunû — the terraced, tree-planted shrine
glossary.Aja: Aja ({d}A.A) — bride of Shamash, the Dawn
glossary.kī: like, as (the short form of kīma)
glossary.gamālum: to spare, deal kindly; gāmil “sparer of”; igmilu “who spared”
glossary.Larsa: Larsa (UD.UNU{ki}) — the sun-god’s southern city, Rim-Sin’s seat
glossary.edēšum: to be new; D-stem uddušum — muddiš “renewer”
glossary.balāṭum: to live; D-stem bulluṭum — muballiṭ “who gives life”
glossary.Uruk: Uruk (UNU{ki}) — city of Anum and Ishtar
glossary.šakānum: to place, establish; šākin “who provides”; aškun “I established”
glossary.elûm: to go up; D-stem ullûm — mulli “who raises high”
glossary.Eanna: Eanna, “House of Heaven” — the great temple of Uruk
glossary.Ištar: Ishtar — goddess of love and war; written {d}INANNA (Harper: Nanâ) or GIŠ.DAR (Harper: Anunit)
glossary.Isin: Isin (NI.SI.IN{ki}) — seat of the dynasty before Babylon’s rise
glossary.daḫādum: to be lavish; D-stem — mudaḫḫid “who makes lavish”
glossary.Egalmaḫ: Egalmah, “Exalted Palace” — Gula’s temple at Isin
glossary.ušumgallum: the ušumgal-dragon (GAL.BÚR); of kings: matchless overlord
glossary.šar ālim: “king of the city” — Harper’s reading of the recurring pair LUGAL.URU-lim
glossary.Zamama: Zamama ({d}ZA.MAL.MAL; modern Zababa) — warrior-god of Kish
glossary.rašādum: to found; Š-stem — mušaršid “who founds firmly”
glossary.Kiš: Kish (KIŠ{ki}) — ancient royal city, Zamama’s seat
glossary.saḫārum: to go around; Š-stem — muštašḫir “who wraps around”
glossary.melammum: the awe-inspiring radiance of gods and kings
glossary.Emeteursag: Emeteursag, “House worthy of the hero” — Zamama’s temple at Kish
glossary.eṣēpum: to arrange duly; muštēṣbi “who arranges in order”
glossary.Ḫursagkalama: Hursag-kalama, “Mountain of the Land” — Kish’s twin sanctuary of Ishtar
glossary.nitraru: helper(?) — Harper’s reading; modern editions see the god Erra, the king’s ally, in these signs
glossary.kašādum: to reach, attain; Š-stem ušakšidu “he let attain”; ikšudu “who reached”
glossary.watārum: to exceed; Š-stem — mušāter “who makes surpassing”; ušāterūšu “they made it supreme”
glossary.Kutû: Cutha ({uru}TIG.GAB.A{ki}, modern reading Gudua) — Nergal’s city of the underworld
glossary.rapāšum: to be broad; D-stem — murappiš “who broadens”
glossary.Meslam: the Meslam — Nergal’s temple at Cutha (Harper: Shid-lam)
glossary.Tutu: Tutu — god of Borsippa, later a name of Marduk
glossary.riāšum: to rejoice; D-stem — murīš “who makes rejoice”
glossary.Ezida: Ezida, “True House” — the temple of Borsippa
glossary.edûm: to know; mūdi “knower of”; īdû “he knows”
glossary.šadālum: to be wide; D-stem — mušaddil “who widens”
glossary.Dilbat: Dilbat (AŠ.BAD{ki}) — farming town south of Babylon, Urash’s city
glossary.garānum: to pile up; D-stem — mugarrin “who heaps up”
glossary.simtum: what befits; simat “the fitting ornament of”
glossary.erištum: the wise one (fem.) — epithet of the mother-goddess
glossary.Mama: Mama ({d}MA.MA) — the mother-goddess who forms kings
glossary.Keš: Kesh — holy city of the mother-goddess (same KIŠ sign; Harper read Kish)
glossary.dešûm: to sprout abundantly; D-stem — mudešši “who supplies richly”
glossary.muštālum: judicious, deliberate (one who takes counsel with himself)
glossary.Lagaš: Lagash (ŠIR.BUR.LA{ki}) — great city of the southeast
glossary.Eninnu: Eninnu, “House Fifty” (É.50) — Ningirsu’s temple at Girsu
glossary.telītum: the Capable One — a title of Ishtar
glossary.Zabalam: Zabalam — Ishtar’s city in the south (Harper copied ṢA.RI.UNU and thought of Aleppo)
glossary.nīšum: raising, lifting; nīš qātim “prayer” (lifting of hands)
glossary.E-udgalgal: E-udgalgal, “House of Great Storms” — Adad’s temple at Karkar
glossary.Adab: Adab (UD.NUN{ki}) — city of the mother-goddess as Ninmah
glossary.ašārum: to organize, care for; āšer “who looks after”
glossary.Emaḫ: Emah, “Exalted House” — the temple at Adab
glossary.maḫārum: to face, withstand; qabal lā maḫārim “combat none can face”
glossary.šaqûm: to give to drink; Š-stem — mušešqi “who waters”
glossary.muttabbilum: capable manager, one who gets things done
glossary.puzzurum: to shelter, hide away; mušpazzir “who gives refuge”
glossary.Malka: Malka (MAL.AL.KA.A{ki}) — Harper’s reading; the city is Malgium on the Tigris
glossary.Ea: Ea ({d}EN.KI) — god of wisdom and the deep
glossary.Damgalnunna: Damgalnunna ({d}DAM.GAL.NUN.NA) — “Great Wife of the Prince,” Ea’s consort
glossary.ṣaltum: warrior(?) — Harper’s reading of a disputed word (also read ittum “by the sign of”)
glossary.Dagan: Dagan — great god of the middle Euphrates
glossary.Mera: Mera ({uru}ME.RA{ki}) — Mari, the great palace-city of the Euphrates
glossary.nawārum: to be bright; D-stem — munawwir “who brightens”
glossary.rēʾûm: shepherd — here written with the Sumerogram SIPA
glossary.wapûm: to appear; Š-stem šūpûm “make appear, make glorious”; mušēpi, ušūpiu
glossary.kittum: truth, right — kittum u mīšarum, “truth and justice”
glossary.ešērum: to be straight; Št-stem šutēšurum “to guide aright”; mušūšer
glossary.ammū: the people (a rare, archaic word; Harper am-mi)
glossary.lamassum: the lamassu — protective guardian spirit (the KAL sign)
glossary.Aššur: Ashur ({uru}A.USAR{ki}) — the city on the Tigris, later Assyria’s heart
glossary.šēbûm: to make shine(?) — Harper’s mu-še-ib-bi; a disputed line
glossary.nabīḫum: radiance(?), rising light(?) — Harper renders “the rising sun(?)”
glossary.Emišmiš: Emishmish (É.MIŠ.MIŠ; Emesmes/Emašmaš) — Ishtar’s temple at Nineveh
glossary.mê-parṣī: the me — the divine rites and powers (me-e)
glossary.waṣûm: to go out; (of light) to rise; Š-stem mušēṣi “who makes go forth”
glossary.aḫāzum: to take, grasp; Š-stem šūḫuzum “to make take hold”
glossary.wârum: to go; D-stem wu’’urum “to commission”: uwaʾʾeranni “he commissioned me”
glossary.pûm: mouth (the KA sign); ina pī mātim “in the mouth of the land”
glossary.inūmišu: “at that time” — the hinge-word of the monument
